role_hints.oews_basis: record's role_mix is 4-digit 5419 and is dominated by veterinary occupations from sibling 541940 (vet techs 9.2%, veterinarians 17.1% of wage bill) — unusable as a proxy for marketing research; the relevant SOCs (13-1161 Market Research Analysts, 19-3022 Survey Researchers, 15-2041 Statisticians, 15-2051 Data Scientists) sit outside the listed top-10. Structure below is hypothesis from industry knowledge, flagged as such
role_hints.note: Questionnaire drafting, coding open-ends, cross-tab analysis, chart production and report writing are already heavily AI-automated (GRIT data shows fast uptake); study design judgment, client counsel and methodological defense persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in the contaminated top-10 — interviewers (43-4111), panel-ops and data-collection staff; hypothesis
role_hints.note: Online panel sampling, quality control and survey programming automate near-fully; CATI phone interviewing is being replaced by AI voice agents; in-person qual (focus-group facilities, ethnography, CLTs) and B2B expert recruitment stay human.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in the contaminated top-10; hypothesis
role_hints.note: AI moderation of async qual and auto-summarization of transcripts is live; senior moderator-client trust and workshop facilitation persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Reps of Services 3.3% appears in the contaminated mix; real firms carry more, hypothesis ~10-15%
role_hints.note: Proposal drafting and RFP responses automate; account relationships with brand insights teams persist and are often personal to founders.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General & Operations Managers 6.6% of wage bill (one of the few uncontaminated signals in the 5419 mix)
role_hints.note: Project P&L tracking and resourcing automate; client escalation and methodology governance persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-4051 Customer Service Reps 2.3% + long tail of admin occupations, est. remainder ~10-15%
role_hints.note: Billing, incentive/honoraria processing, scheduling and compliance paperwork automate near-fully.

source_hints.why: The standard industry survey; recent editions carry detailed AI-adoption data (synthetic respondents, AI analysis tools) — the best single source for current_adoption_pct.

source_hints.source: Insights Association surveys and IDEA Forum materials
source_hints.why: US trade association (CASRO+MRA merger); primary member data on AI use, revenue trends and firm demographics.
Global industry sizing with US split and commentary on pricing/segment shifts (established research vs. data-analytics substitutes).

source_hints.source: AAPOR (American Association for Public Opinion Research) standards and polling-industry commentary
source_hints.why: For the polling sub-segment: methodological standards and the documented response-rate crisis that squeezes traditional polling economics.

source_hints.source: Oberon / Equiteq-style research & analytics M&A commentary; SI (Significant Insights) or MrWeb industry deal coverage
source_hints.why: Deal activity in insights (e.g., Behaviorally, Savanta, STRAT7 build-ups) is covered by niche trade press; named advisors here are uncertain — verify who currently publishes insights-sector deal data.

source_hints.source: Insights Association member demographics / GRIT firm-size data
source_hints.why: Founder-led boutiques dominate the small end (2,509 of 3,747 firms <5 employees); association data is the plausible owner-age source.

capture_questions: Research is sold as fixed-price project bids (and some retainers/trackers) against competitive RFPs — when AI collapses the cost of programming, fieldwork QC, analysis and reporting, does bid competition pass the savings to clients as lower project prices (the deflation channel), or do trusted-methodology relationships let incumbents hold price and expand margin?

Who owns the client relationship — the agency, or the individual senior researcher whose methodological credibility the insights buyer actually trusts? How much revenue survives a founder's exit or a key director's departure?

What stops a brand insights team from bypassing agencies entirely — using LLMs plus a panel-access subscription (or synthetic respondents) to run studies in-house — and what evidence exists that this insourcing is already happening at the small-study end?

Tracker studies and long-running brand trackers are the recurring-revenue base — are they contractually sticky, or are they the first line item clients re-bid when AI-native competitors quote 50% less?
terminal_value_question: Distinguish AI cutting the researcher's costs from AI replacing the researcher's product: if synthetic respondents and LLM analysis of existing data become credible substitutes for primary fieldwork, the product itself (paid access to real human respondents plus trusted interpretation) shrinks — the deciding mechanism is methodological trust: do buyers (and their legal/regulatory users of research, e.g. claims substantiation, litigation surveys) keep requiring verifiably human samples, or does 'good enough' synthetic data hollow out paid demand? A researcher could wrongly score this as a pure cost-cut winner without noticing the substitution.
special_notes: Role-mix contamination is total: the 4-digit 5419 proxy is dominated by veterinary occupations (541940 is the giant sibling) — vet techs, vet assistants and veterinarians head the list. Do not use these shares; the role_hints above are explicitly hypothesis-based, and the run should sanity-check against OES 13-1161/19-3022 concentrations if it can.

Pricing-model deflation candidate: fixed-price competitive bidding on projects; AI-native entrants are already quoting dramatically lower. Model pi_dist with the bid-competition channel explicit, and treat tracker/retainer revenue separately (stickier).

Melter watch: this code carries product-substitution risk (synthetic respondents, DIY platforms like Qualtrics/SurveyMonkey siphoning the low end for 15 years already) — the DIY precedent is the base rate for what AI does next. Do not let strong current margins mask the trajectory.

Boundary confusion: data analytics consultancies sit in 541511/541611/518210, DIY survey-software platforms are 511210/518210, public-opinion polling nonprofits (Pew) are out of scope, and big holders (Nielsen, Kantar, Ipsos, Gartner) straddle codes — ESOMAR/GRIT 'industry' figures include tech/platform revenue not in 541910; check coverage before quoting market size against the code's $26.7B receipts.

Structure: 78 firms with 500+ employees hold a large revenue share (avg $191M) — the roll-up-relevant universe is the 20-99 class (309 firms) and 100-499 class (120 firms); n_band 429 uses a generic 15.65% margin, plausible for insights boutiques but verify against any published agency-margin benchmarks.

Polling sub-segment is economically marginal (response-rate collapse, election-cycle lumpiness) and reputationally distinct — do not let polling headlines drive scores for the much larger commercial research base.
